We moved to the country so that the children would have a garden However, relative pronouns are possible with preposition structures.

I can't think of anybody to invite, (     .. .anybody whom to invite.) pronoun is not normally used.

When  a noun or pronoun is the object of a following infinitive, a relative 14 relative      + infinitive:      a  garden        in   which       to   play not You're the one that knows where to go. (     .. .the one that know...) responsible ...) It's me that's responsible for the organisation. (More formal: It is I who am especially in an informal style.

clauses of cleft sentences (►  273). However, the verb is usually third-person, A  different kind of first- and second-person reference is common in the relative we who ... are unusual, though they sometimes occur in a very formal style.

Most relative clauses have third-person reference; I who ..., you who ... and 13 agreement        of  person I called in Mrs Spencer, the Manager's secretary, who did our accounts.

relative pronoun.

However, a descriptive phrase can sometimes separate a noun from its Mrs Spencer in,  who did our accounts.) I called in Mrs Spencer, who did our accounts, (NOT    I called which she put forward.) not The idea which she put forward was interesting, (     The idea was interesting Relative pronouns usually follow their nouns directly.

12 separating        a  noun    from    its   relative      pronoun Tuesday's the only date possible.

Please send me all the tickets available. (= ... that are available.) Reduced structures are also used with the adjectives available and possible.

I found him  sitting at a table covered with papers. (= ... which  was covered (= ... who  were invited ...) Half of the people invited to the party didn't turn up.

Anyone touching that wire will get a shock. (= ... who  touches ...) your brother?) Who's the girl dancing with your brother? (= . .. that is dancing with A  participle is often used instead of a relative pronoun and full verb.

to see again.

I've just been to see an old friend that I'm not sure when I'm going However, this sometimes happens in informal speech.

We   do not usually combine a relative clause with an indirect question structure.

18  a  car    that     I didn't      know       how     fast     it  could      go,   etc sign and return.

I am enclosing an application form, which I should be grateful if you would following.

Relative clauses can also be combined with if-clauses in sentences like the (More correct:... who we hope will be...) This is a letter from my father, whom we hope will be fully recovered soon.

generally considered correct.

In this structure, people sometimes use whom as a subject pronoun. This is not not (     This is the woman (who/that) Anna said that could show us...) This is the woman (who/that) Anna said could show us the church.

it must be dropped if the relative pronoun is a subject.

Note that the conjunction (the second that) is usually dropped in this structure; That's the man (who/that) I wish (that) I'd married.

It's a house (which/that) we feel (that) we might want to buy.

We're going to meet somebody (who/that) I know (that) you'll like.

relative pronoun.

informal style. What I need is a long rest.

Relative clauses (including what-clauses) are used to structure 'cleft sentences': 16 It was     Alice     who     .. What      I need      is  ...

be centrally important to the overall message. When  this is not so, non­ In general, identifying clauses are used when the information they give is felt to or (   We became friendly with some nurses, whom Jack had met in Paris.) We became friendly with some nurses that Jack had met in Paris.
